coredata.dc:title: Biocompatible gelatin/carbon dot nanocomposite based urea sensor and the effect of nitrogen ion implantation
coredata.dc:creator: Priyadarshini, Eepsita
Minzar, Mohd
Pandey, Saurabh
Rawat, Kamla
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we have fabricated a novel platform for sensing of urea using gelatin/carbon dots nanocomposite system. The sensor electrode was created by depositing the nanocomposite gel onto thin glass plates coated with indium tin oxide (ITO) using the drop casting technique. The behavior of these electrodes was investigated against a number of bioanalytes in the concentration range of 2–20 mM by cyclic voltammetry. The system was observed to be highly selective for urea with a sensitivity of 1.65 μA/mM/cm in the experimental linear range of 2–20 mM. Furthermore, the gelatin/CD-ITO electrode were also subjected to 50 KeV N2 + ion beam irradiation with varying fluence in the range of 1012 to 1016 ions/cm2. Sensing profile of the irradiated samples for urea suggested enhancement in sensitivity to 2 μA/mM cm2, when the ion fluence was 5 × 1015 ions/cm2. This enhancement after irradiation suggests a clear dependence of detection on the fluence of the ion beam. The observed excellent sensitivity of radiation processed nanocomposite material can be used as an enzyme-free platform for urea detection. Additionally, the CDs showed fluorescence quenching on treatment with mere 50 μM urea suggesting the high sensitivity of the platform.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Urea
Carbon dots
Cyclic voltammetry
Ion implantation
Electrochemical sensing
Enzyme-free sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Advanced Instrument Research Facility of the Jawaharlal Nehru University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors express their gratitude to the Advanced Instrument Research Facility of the Jawaharlal Nehru University for providing access to the TEM facility. KR acknowledges the support received from ICMR (35/11/2019-Nano/BMS) and UGC-startup, Government of India through a research grant. The authors would also like to acknowledge the Advanced Research Instrumentation Facility (AIRF) of the Jawaharlal Nehru University for granting access to the SEM and FTIR facility, as well as the Inter University Accelerator Centre (IUAC) for providing the nitrogen ion beam irradiation facility. Special thanks are extended to Dr. K. Asokan and Dr. D. Kanjilal from IUAC, and Prof. H. B. Bohidar from SPS, JNU for their valuable discussions.

The authors express their gratitude to the Advanced Instrument Research Facility of the Jawaharlal Nehru University for providing access to the TEM facility. KR acknowledges the support received from ICMR ( 35/11/2019-Nano/BMS ) and UGC-startup, Government of India through a research grant. The authors would also like to acknowledge the Advanced Research Instrumentation Facility (AIRF) of the Jawaharlal Nehru University for granting access to the SEM and FTIR facility, as well as the Inter University Accelerator Centre (IUAC) for providing the nitrogen ion beam irradiation facility. Special thanks are extended to Dr. K. Asokan and Dr. D. Kanjilal from IUAC, and Prof. H. B. Bohidar from SPS, JNU for their valuable discussions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A). The cyclic voltammograms of the gelatin/CD electrode were recorded while varying the concentration of carbon dots (CDs). The inset graph illustrates the changes in anodic and cathodic currents as a function of CD concentration, revealing maximum sensitivity at a concentration of 0.05%. (B) The cyclic voltammograms of the gelatin/CD electrode were recorded at various scan rates. The inset graph demonstrates that the peak current exhibited a linear relationship with the square root of the scan rate, with a high goodness-of-fit value (χ2) of 0.99.

(A). Cyclic voltammograms of gelatin/CD in presence of varying concentration of urea analyte (B) The relationship between the current and urea concentration displayed a linear pattern, indicating the sensitivity of the system within the linear range of 2–20 mM urea. (C) The current potential response of the gelatin/CD-ITO electrode to urea was evaluated in the presence of individual interfering analytes, each at a concentration of 10 mM. The inset graph illustrates the impact of other analytes on the sensing of urea, depicted as a variation in current. (D) The graph displays the relationship between the anodic and cathodic peak current and the ion fluences of N2 + ions, indicating the dependence of current on the intensity of ion irradiation.

(A). Cyclic voltammagraphs of (a) anodic (b) cathodic pea current with respect to urea concentration in the range of 2–20 mM for samples irradiated with different ion beam fluence. (B) Schematic illustration of the mechanism of electrochemical sensing of urea by Gelatin/CD-ITO eletrode.

The graph illustrates the sensitivity of the gelatin/CD-ITO electrode at various ion fluences, demonstrating that the electrode irradiated at a fluence of 5 × 1015 ions/cm2 exhibited the highest sensitivity.

(A). UV–Visible absorbance and (B) Fluorescence spectra (λex = 354 nm) of carbon dots on 54treatment with varying concentration of urea. Fig. 5 (C) &(D). FTIR spectra of gelatin/CD-ITO electrode (C) low fluence irradiated samples before and after treating with urea (D) high fluence irradiated samples before and after treating with urea.

head.ce:title: Biocompatible gelatin/carbon dot nanocomposite based urea sensor and the effect of nitrogen ion implantation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physical Sciences, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physical Sciences, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Chemical and Life Sciences, Jamia Hamdard, New Delhi, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Life Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Chemical and Life Sciences, Jamia Hamdard, New Delhi, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry, School of Chemical and Life Sciences, Jamia Hamdard, New Delhi, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Life Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biochemistry, School of Chemical and Life Sciences, Jamia Hamdard, New Delhi, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we have fabricated a novel platform for sensing of urea using gelatin/carbon dots nanocomposite system. The sensor electrode was created by depositing the nanocomposite gel onto thin glass plates coated with indium tin oxide (ITO) using the drop casting technique. The behavior of these electrodes was investigated against a number of bioanalytes in the concentration range of 2–20 mM by cyclic voltammetry. The system was observed to be highly selective for urea with a sensitivity of 1.65 μA/mM/cm in the experimental linear range of 2–20 mM. Furthermore, the gelatin/CD-ITO electrode were also subjected to 50 KeV N2 + ion beam irradiation with varying fluence in the range of 1012 to 1016 ions/cm2. Sensing profile of the irradiated samples for urea suggested enhancement in sensitivity to 2 μA/mM cm2, when the ion fluence was 5 × 1015 ions/cm2. This enhancement after irradiation suggests a clear dependence of detection on the fluence of the ion beam. The observed excellent sensitivity of radiation processed nanocomposite material can be used as an enzyme-free platform for urea detection. Additionally, the CDs showed fluorescence quenching on treatment with mere 50 μM urea suggesting the high sensitivity of the platform.

ce:section.ce:para: Urea apart from being an essential fertilizer represents an important group of biomolecules that play an integral role in protein processing and human metabolic pathways. The presence of urea beyond a threshold in blood or urine may be treated as a marker in the diagnosis of renal and liver diseases. While the serum urea level in a healthy individual lies in the range of 2.5–6.7 mM, in patients with renal diseases the serum urea concentration increases to 30–80 mM [1]. Hence, in medical care sector, the amount of urea in blood or urine is used for determining the healthy condition of kidney. Additionally, high urea level is associated with dehydration, gastrointestinal damage, shock while lower amount of urea is associated with the risk of hepatic failure, nephritic disease and cachexia [2]. As already stated urea is an essential fertilizer and hence it's also crucial to estimate urea level in drinking water supply, food products and other agro-industrial samples. Thus, detection and sensing of urea is a necessity both from medical, nutritional and environmental perspectives.

To date there have been a number of articles that have focused on the urea detection using techniques such as ion exchange chromatography, fluorimetric analysis and electrochemical biosensing [3]. Most of these methods involve an enzymatic mode of detection [4–7] or immunoassay [8,9] involving the use of urease/glutamate dehydrogenase enzyme. Though these methods offer the advantage of sensitivity, are associated with numerous drawbacks such as requirement of low temperature, stringent handling conditions, costly and complex sample preparation procedure. Pulsed-amperometric method is another preferred approach for urea detection and involves the fabrication of the biosensors based on the incorporation of appropriate enzymes into conducting polymers (such as polypyrrole). But this method involves several standardization steps such as the amount of enzyme required for enhancement of detection limit [10]. In addition several non-enzymatic electrochemical methods have also been reported for urea detection. A potentiodynamic polymerized pyrrole film was designed by Mondal and Sangaranarayanan [11] that worked under mild acidic conditions with a sensitivity of 1.11 μA μM−1 cm−1. Similarly, a Pt based electrode working under alkaline condition was developed as urea sensor by Patzer et al. [12] though electrochemical analysis has the advantages of simplicity, high sensitivity, low cost and fast reaction compared to other methods being used recently, these sensors discussed above are pH dependent and hence, can provide erroneous results when it comes to detection in body fluids, and food samples at near neutral pH. Because of the disadvantages associated with some sensors developed so far, there is an urgent need to develop a new sensing platform for urea detection that is rapid, biocompatible, cost-effective and working at neutral pH and enzyme-free.

Apart from this, nanomaterials such as quantum dots (QDs) and semiconductor nanomaterials have been reported for detection of urea. Huang et al. [13] reported the use of mercaptosuccinic acid (MSA)-QDs and urease for urea detection. Though the process had the advantage of water solubility, it suffers from toxicity issues and requires high sample amount for optical and photoluminescence based detection. Except for the toxicity issue, QDs can be a superior class of novel materials whose physico-chemical properties make them excellent substrates for detection of specific analytes. Therefore, with minor modifications and under appropriate conditions, biocompatible nanomaterials can be prepared for use in detection and sensing of analytes. With this in view, we have synthesized biocompatible and water soluble carbon dots (CDs) [14] and studied their potential application in electrochemical based urea sensing.

In this study, we have used gelatin/CD nanocomposites as substrate for sensing of urea in aqueous samples. The notable aspect of this study is the simplicity of the system and the use of a non-enzymatic mode of detection, which contributes to the ease of implementation and cost-effectiveness of the approach. In addition, we have also studied the influence of low energy (50 KeV) N2 + ion beam of various fluence to process the nanocomposite and subsequently analyzed their urea detection efficiency. Radiation processing brings about surface alteration in the morphological as well as electrochemical properties of the electrode. We have, thus established a comparative analysis of non-irradiated and irradiated gelatin/CD ITO electrode efficiency as urea sensor and examined their relative sensitivity against urea. To the best of our knowledge this is the first instance of employing carbon dots system for the detection of urea in aqueous samples.

The materials and reagents utilized in the study were acquired from Sigma Aldrich, USA. For the investigation, Gelatin-B (derived from Type B bovine skin extract, 225 bloom, pI ∼ 5.1 ± 0.1) with a molecular weight of 50 KDa was employed. All other chemicals employed were of analytical grade. The reagents were prepared using deionized water without any additional purification. Indium-tin-oxide (ITO) coated glass plates were obtained from Balzers, UK (specifically, Baltracom 247 ITO, 1.1 mm thick), which had a sheet resistance of 25 Ω sq-1 and a transmittance of 90%.
Citric acid and polyethylene glycol were used as precursors in CD synthesis. CDs were synthesized and characterized as per the prior standardized procedure.14

The preparation of gelatin/CD-ITO electrodes was conducted as follows. A gelatin solution was initially created by dissolving 2.5% (w/v) gelatin powder in deionized water. The solution was then stirred continuously at 40 °C for 30 min to ensure homogeneity and clarity. Subsequently, synthesized CDs were added to the gelatin solution, resulting in a gelatin/CD suspension with a final CD volume concentration of 0.05% (w/v). The suspension was stirred continuously at 40 °C for 30 min to facilitate thorough mixing of the CDs and gelatin.

To create the thin film of the gelatin/CD-ITO electrode, a drop casting method was employed. Specifically, 50 μl of the gelatin/CD suspension was uniformly dispersed onto an ITO film, which covered an area of 0.25 cm2. Subsequently, the films were allowed to air dry at ambient temperature (25 °C) for approximately 24 h. The electrochemical performance of the fabricated electrodes was closely monitored, revealing that the highest current was observed at a CD concentration of 0.05% (w/v). With the exception of a few experiments involving the standardization of CD concentration, all other experiments were conducted using thin films composed of 2.5% (w/v) gelatin B and 0.05% (w/v) CD.

The electrochemical sensing of analytes was carried out through Cyclic voltammetry (CV) studies using an Autolab Potentiostat/Galvanostat (Eco Chemie, Netherlands). A three-electrode cell setup was utilized, comprising the Gelatin/CD-ITO working electrode, a platinum wire auxiliary electrode, and an Ag/AgCl reference electrode. The CV experiments were conducted within the potential range of −0.05 to 0.5 V, using Zobell's solution (3.3 mM K4Fe(CN)6, 3.3 mM K3Fe(CN)6 with 0.1 M KCl at pH 7) as the electrolyte. To ensure standardization, various parameters such as scan rate, CD concentration, and analyte concentration were systematically adjusted by performing the experiments thrice under identical conditions.

The analytes investigated in the study included urea (U), ascorbic acid (AA), oxalic acid (OA), gallic acid (GA), cholesterol (Chox), dextrose (D), L-cysteine (Cys), and citric acid (CA). Stock solutions of these analytes, each with a concentration of 20 mM, were individually prepared in deionized water and stored at 4 °C when not in use.

In order to investigate the influence of ion irradiation on the sensing performance, the gelatin/CD-ITO thin films were exposed to nitrogen molecular ion (N2+) beam irradiation at different fluences, ranging from 1012 to 1016 ions/cm2. The ion beam irradiations were performed utilizing the low-energy beam facility at IUAC (Inter University Accelerator Center, New Delhi, India), employing a 50 keV N2 + ion beam [15].

Both the Gelatin/CD-ITO and irradiated Gelatin/CD-ITO electrodes were employed for the electrochemical profiling of the aforementioned analytes. The results indicated that the Gelatin/CD-ITO electrodes exhibited selectivity only towards urea. Additionally, spectroscopic analysis (including optical and fluorescence) was conducted to detect urea using the same CDs. For this purpose, dispersions of synthesized CDs (1.9 mg/ml) were treated with incrementally increasing concentrations of urea, and the UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectra were recorded.

The electron transfer kinetics of the electrode were examined through cyclic voltammetry (CV). Before conducting the detection study on analytes, optimization of parameters such as scan rate and CD concentration was performed. Subsequently, electrochemical investigations were carried out on various analytes including urea, ascorbic acid, oxalic acid, gallic acid, cholesterol, dextrose, L-cysteine, and citric acid, covering a concentration range of 2–20 mM.

The electrochemical behavior of the gelatin/CD-ITO electrode was monitored within the potential range of −0.05 to 0.5 V. Since the concentration of CD in the mixture had a notable impact on the electrochemical properties, our initial focus was to examine the influence of varying CD concentrations on the peak current. Fig. 1 (A) illustrates the CV profile of the gelatin/CD-ITO electrode with different CD concentrations. The inset in Fig. 1(A) displays an increase in the cathodic current at a CD concentration of 0.05% (w/v), which then remained relatively constant before gradually decreasing. Conversely, the anodic current (Ia) exhibited the opposite trend. Notably, the highest peak currents (Ia and Ic) were observed at a CD concentration of 0.05%, indicating superior electron transfer properties and sensitivity compared to other concentrations. Therefore, all subsequent experiments were conducted under these conditions.

Additionally, we examined the CV profiles of the bare ITO electrode and the gelatin/CD-ITO electrode at a scan rate of 50 mV/s in Zobell's solution. The results revealed that the peak current of the gelatin/CD-ITO electrode was lower compared to the bare ITO electrode (Fig. S1, Supporting Information). This discrepancy suggested the presence of a film on the ITO surface, which hindered electron transfer accessibility in comparison to the ITO electrode [16,17].

Subsequently, the impact of scan rate on the peak current was examined. Fig. 1(B) depicts the cyclic voltammograms of the gelatin/CD-ITO electrode within the potential range of −0.05 to 0.5 V, with varying scan rates ranging from 10 to 50 mV/s. It can be observed that an increase in peak current occurred as the scan rate was increased. Well-defined peaks were formed at different scan rates, exhibiting a linear rise in peak current intensity. The inset of Fig. 1(B) illustrates the linear relationship between the anodic and cathodic peak currents and the square root of the scan rate, with χ2 of 0.98 and 0.99, respectively. This indicates that both the anodic and cathodic peak currents exhibited a linear dependence on the square root of the scan rate. Therefore, it can be concluded that the electrochemical reaction followed a diffusion-driven process. Furthermore, it was observed that the peak separation potential remained constant regardless of the scan rate. This finding suggests that a diffusion-controlled redox reaction took place within the gelatin/CD-ITO electrode.

The main objective of this study was to evaluate the potential of the gelatin/CD electrode for bioanalyte detection. To achieve this, we examined the electrochemical behavior of the gelatin/CD electrode towards various bioanalytes in the concentration range of 2–20 mM, using a fixed scan rate of 50 mV/s. The changes in anodic and cathodic peak currents were observed to determine the electrode's response to the tested bioanalytes. A single gelatin/CD-ITO electrode was used for each concentration range of a specific analyte. Notable alterations in the cyclic voltammogram (CV) profiles were observed specifically in the case of urea, while no significant changes were observed for the other tested analytes, even at high concentrations of 20 mM (data not shown).

In the case of urea, an increase in the anodic peak current was observed with increasing urea concentration. Furthermore, there was a shift of the anodic peak current towards higher potentials as the urea concentration increased. Fig. 2 (A) illustrates the CV of the gelatin/CD-ITO electrode treated with varying concentrations of urea, clearly indicating an increase in oxidation (Ia) and reduction (Ic) currents upon exposure to urea. This suggests that the observed electrocatalytic reaction was a result of the presence of urea. Conversely, no significant changes in peak current were observed for the other bioanalytes, even at a high concentration of 20 mM.

By plotting the anodic and cathodic peak currents against the urea concentration, a linear relationship was observed with a high goodness-of-fit (χ2) value of 0.97 and 0.98, respectively (Fig. 2(B)). The linear detection range for urea was determined to be 2–20 mM, and the sensitivity of the electrode towards urea was calculated from the slope of the curve, resulting in a value of 1.65 μA/mM/cm2. Table 1 provides a comparative analysis of different sensing platforms for urea detection, including their sensitivity. The data suggests that our system exhibits a significantly higher sensitivity compared to existing enzymatic and non-enzymatic detection methods.

In enzymatic mode detection, the presence of the urease enzyme is required for the catalytic hydrolysis of urea and the measurement of ions involved or produced during the process. In contrast, the present method proposes a non-enzymatic approach where changes in electrochemical current were observed upon treating the electrode with increasing concentrations of urea. This non-enzymatic process resembles the enzymatic reaction, as the interaction with the system leads to the decomposition of urea, resulting in the generation of NH3. Subsequently, NH3 is converted to NH4 +, which is responsible for the observed increase in peak current [22]. Scheme (Fig. 3 (B)) presents a representative mechanism illustrating the detection of urea by the Gelatin/CD-ITO electrode.

For determining the selectivity of the gelatin/CD-ITO electrode, we monitored the CV profile in presence of interfering analytes. For this urea and the interfering analytes were used in 1:1 ratio, and the electrochemical changes were recorded. The concentration of urea and interfering analyte was 10 mM. We observed that the present system was quite sensitive for urea and no significant changes were observed in presence of other interfering analytes (Fig. 2(C)). The inset in Fig. 2(C) demonstrates the impact of these analytes on the anodic current profile. It is noteworthy that no significant alterations in the peak current were observed, even in the presence of other analytes. Therefore, the gelatin/CD-ITO electrode exhibited exceptional selectivity specifically towards urea.

To investigate the effect of nitrogen molecular ion (N2 +) beam irradiation on the sensing profile of gelatin/CD-ITO electrode, the electrodes were irradiated at 50 KeV with fluences of 1012, 5 × 1012, 1013, 5 × 1013, 1014, 5 × 1014, 1015, 5 × 1015, 1016 and 5 × 1016 ions/cm2. The CV studies of the irradiated electrodes were then performed in the potential range of −0.05 to 0.5 V. Fig. 2(D) shows the electrochemical response of electrode after irradiation with ions of variant fluences. It was observed that the electrodes irradiated with fluence N2 + ≤ 1015ions/cm2 showed an increase in anodic and cathodic peak current compared to the non-irradiated gelatin/CD-ITO electrode. However, the electrodes irradiated with high fluence N2 + > 1015 ions/cm2 showed a decrease in anodic and cathodic peak current compared to non-irradiated electrodes. Irradiation resulted in the incorporation of N2 + ions into the gelatin/CD-ITO electrode, and therefore, a variation in the peak current was observed with dose of fluence. Ion implantation may have caused a change in the charge transfer of electrodes, and this is why there was a variation in the peak current magnitude on irradiation.

For analyzing the effect of irradiation on sensitivity, the irradiated electrodes were treated with urea in the concentration range of 2–20 mM, keeping all other parameters same as before. In all the cases an increase in the anodic peak current was noticed with urea concentration (Fig. 3). An increase in current with urea concentration suggested that there was interaction of the irradiated electrodes with urea. Maximum increase in the magnitude of peak current for urea sample was observed in electrodes irradiated with high fluence N2 + > 1015 ions/cm2. This suggests that in presence of urea, an easy conducting path for electron transfer from electrode to electrolyte was established. Next, we calculated the sensitivity of the electrodes for urea detection from the slope of the plots of peak current versus urea concentration. Fig. 4
shows the sensitivity of the electrodes irradiated with variant fluences. Most probably the bombardment of N2 + ions at high fluence resulted in increased accessibility to active sites required for the hydrolysis of urea. This resulted in high transfer of electrolytes to the electrode and the resultant increase in sensitivity. Thus, CV analysis suggested that high fluence irradiated electrode showed enhancement in electron transfer and assisted in increasing the sensitivity towards urea detection.

We performed optical and fluorescence analysis to evaluate the ability of CDs to detect urea. On treating with increasing concentration of urea, slight blue shifting of the peak at 300 nm, which was the characteristic absorption peak of CDs was observed. Similarly, quenching of fluorescence was observed on treatment with increasing concentration of urea. The results suggest that the synthesized CDs have the potential to efficiently detect urea up to a very low concentration of 50 μM by fluorescence spectroscopy.

FTIR spectroscopy in the frequency range of 400–4000 cm−1 was employed to examine the changes in the gelatin/CD-ITO electrodes resulting from the binding of urea. Additionally, morphological and chemical changes in the irradiated electrodes were investigated by analyzing the variations in peak intensity. The non-irradiated gelatin/CD-ITO electrode exhibited a broad band at 3072 cm−1 and a weak band at 2360 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of O–H and C–H, respectively, which originated from the precursor citric acid and PEG used in CD synthesis. Upon treatment with urea, distinct alterations in the spectra were observed, including the disappearance or reduction in intensity of certain peaks, indicating an interaction between the electrode and urea.

This study aimed to develop a gelatin/CD system where CD was incorporated into the gelatin matrix. The thin film of this gelatin/CD nanocomposite was uniformly deposited and dried on an ITO coated glass plate, and used as electrode for highly selective and sensitive detection of urea. The electrode exhibited a very high sensitivity of 1.65 μA mM−1 cm−2 in the linear range of 2–20 mM urea. Additionally, N2 + ion beam irradiation of the gelatin/CD-ITO electrode was done and the irradiated electrodes were used for urea sensing. Irradiation of ion beam with high fluence, resulted in increased accessibility to the active sites required for hydrolyzing urea that assisted in enhanced sensitivity for urea at fluence of 5 × 1015 ions/cm2. We thus inferred that irradiation introduced tenability, and induced sensitivity, and alteration in electrochemical properties of the electrode. This sensing system offers a novel platform for enzyme-free and highly sensitive detection of urea, showcasing potential applications in real-time detection of clinical samples.
KR conceptualized and planned the experiments, EP and MM conducted the experiments, KR and SP analyzed the data, and KR and SP wrote the paper.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: Authors declare no conflict of Interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors express their gratitude to the Advanced Instrument Research Facility of the Jawaharlal Nehru University for providing access to the TEM facility. KR acknowledges the support received from ICMR (35/11/2019-Nano/BMS) and UGC-startup, Government of India through a research grant. The authors would also like to acknowledge the Advanced Research Instrumentation Facility (AIRF) of the Jawaharlal Nehru University for granting access to the SEM and FTIR facility, as well as the Inter University Accelerator Centre (IUAC) for providing the nitrogen ion beam irradiation facility. Special thanks are extended to Dr. K. Asokan and Dr. D. Kanjilal from IUAC, and Prof. H. B. Bohidar from SPS, JNU for their valuable discussions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1
Supporting Information is available free of charge. Comparison of cyclic voltammograms profile of bare ITO and gelatin/CD at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. It is general condition followed during the experimental measurement is available in the supplementary information.
Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bbrc.2023.06.081.
